title: Long-term effects of redlining on climate risk exposure
data_sources: HOLC redlining maps (Mapping Inequality, Univ. of Richmond)
tree canopy / surface permeability / foundation height layers
historical census from Aaronson et al. 2021 (restricted)

data_scope.design_unit: parcels on either side of HOLC grade boundaries
data_scope.climate_outcomes: current and projected flood risk; extreme-heat exposure
data_scope.mechanisms: tree canopy, ground-surface permeability, construction foundation height
data_scope.restricted_data: First Street flood data and Aaronson et al. (2021) historical census not publicly shareable

core_conclusion: Neighborhoods graded as less desirable by the Home Owners' Loan Corporation (redlining) in the 1930s-1940s face disproportionately higher current and projected exposure to flooding and extreme heat. A boundary-discontinuity design across 202 US cities attributes this partly to diminished environmental capital: less tree canopy, lower surface permeability, and lower foundation height. Historical policy still shapes who is most vulnerable to climate hazards today.
why_it_matters: Connects historical injustice to present climate vulnerability with a quasi-experimental boundary design; identifies physical mechanisms (canopy, permeability, foundation height) rather than only the disparity; addresses both current and projected risk; supplies concrete place-based adaptation levers.
why_nature: Couples space, history, and human well-being; generalizable multi-city evidence (202 cities) with a unified methodology; causal credibility via boundary discontinuity; highly policy-translatable for urban adaptation and reparative planning.
added_value_to_my_research: Mediation/decomposition at boundaries -> separate structural legacy from contemporary confounders in explainable spatial models
Fuse HOLC outcomes with GPS trajectories -> person-centered spatiotemporal heat/flood exposure for health-access models
Reparative greening as equity-weighted facility-location optimization (canopy as charger analog)
Port boundary-discontinuity design to Global South zoning/slum boundaries for cross-regional equity analysis
Use redlining boundaries as a built-in sampling-bias stress test for FAIR data fusion and geospatial foundation models
